<!-- Topic 2: Recovering From Bad cin Input -->
<!-- Slides 18-28 -->

# Recovering From Bad cin Input
<!-- Slide 18 -->

## When Input Breaks The Stream {.smaller}

- If the client enters text where an `int` is expected, extraction fails.
- The variable does not receive usable data.

::: notes
Slides 18-28
:::

<!-- Slide 19 -->

---

## Range Failure vs Type Failure

| Failure | What Happened |
|---|---|
| Range failure | The value was read, but it was outside the allowed range |
| Type failure | The value could not be read as the requested type |

<!-- Slide 20 -->

---

## Detecting Failed Extraction

```cpp
int score;

cout << "Score: ";
if (!(cin >> score)) {
    cout << "That was not a whole number." << endl;
}
```

<!-- Slide 21 -->

---

## Why The Same Bad Input Repeats

- Failed extraction does not automatically remove the bad characters.
- The next read sees the same bad input again.
- The program must repair the stream before trying another read.

<!-- Slide 22 -->

---

## What cin.clear() Fixes

- `cin.clear()` resets the stream's error state.
- It tells `cin` that future reads are allowed to happen.
- It does not remove the bad input by itself.

<!-- Slide 23 -->

---

## What cin.ignore() Removes

- `cin.ignore()` discards characters waiting in the input buffer.
- Validation code usually discards the rest of the line.
- After ignoring the bad input, the next prompt can read fresh data.

<!-- Slide 24 -->

---

## The Recovery Pattern

```cpp
int score;
cout << "Score: ";

while (!(cin >> score)) {
    cin.clear();
    cin.ignore(10000, '\n');
    cout << "Enter a whole number: ";
}
```

<!-- Slide 25 -->

---

## Combining Type And Range Checks

```cpp
int score;
cout << "Score: ";

while (!(cin >> score) || score < 0 || score > 100) {
    cin.clear();
    cin.ignore(10000, '\n');
    cout << "Enter a whole number from 0 to 100: ";
}
```

::: notes
This compact pattern is easier to accept after students understand the two separate failure modes.
:::

<!-- Slide 26 -->

---

## Common Practices

- Keep the recovery message specific.
- Discard the rest of the line after a failed extraction.
- Do not use a value until the read succeeds.

<!-- Slide 27 -->

---

## Summary

- Bad values can be rejected.
- Bad extractions must be repaired before the program can keep reading.

<!-- Slide 28 -->
